# Questions in Livestream 184

Live Stream #184: Priesthood of the Most Sacred Question

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=660" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:00</a> | What are good ways to encourage other Christians to make culture, such as books or songs? | He recommends small creative groups, like writing circles or Christ-centered workshops, that help Christians develop better creative work without forcing it on people. | He suggests forming small creative groups the way churches form Bible studies: writing circles, art groups, public-speaking groups, or other focused workshops. The point is mutual encouragement, critique, and development of ideas so Christians learn to make stronger work in community rather than simply being told to produce culture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=904" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:04</a> | On a scale where the author should be forced to retire, what is the average recent book he has encountered? | He says recent fiction has mostly been awful in both morality and quality, with only one good novel among the last several he read. | He says his recent reading has been extremely poor, estimating that only one of roughly the last fifteen novels he read was good, and even that author was not broadly recommendable. His criticism is not only moral; he says the writing quality itself is bad and seems to go along with the moral decline. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=996" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:36</a> | Are there any good ancient Egyptian historical fiction books? | He says nothing he has read in the last ten years qualifies, and that the genre is hard to do well because few writers understand the culture well enough. | He qualifies that he has not been a heavy reader of Egyptian historical fiction, preferring science fiction and fantasy, but nothing he has read in the last decade would count as good. He says the genre is especially difficult because the writer has to understand ancient Egyptian culture well enough to make the setting work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=1207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:07</a> | Is Revelation future or past, was Nero the Antichrist, and what is his view of the Rapture? | He gives short answers: he is futurist on Revelation, rejects Nero as the Antichrist, and keeps the Rapture discussion brief. | He says he is a futurist because he thinks Revelation was written after Jerusalem's destruction in AD 70, so he does not accept the semi-preterist reading, though he does not call it heretical. He distinguishes Nero as an antichrist or type rather than the Antichrist, and defines the Rapture as the summary judgment of the reprobate before Christ's second coming. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=1349" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:29</a> | Is limited atonement biblical, especially in light of 1 John 2:2? | He argues that Christ's atonement is sufficient for the world but effective for believers, so he sees limited atonement as the better conclusion. | He contrasts unlimited atonement, which he describes as Christ having absolutely wiped out all human sin, with limited atonement, where Christ's sacrifice has potential sufficiency for all but is effective only for believers. He reads 1 John 2:2 alongside passages such as 1 John 5 and Jeremiah 31, arguing that unbelievers still die for their own sins, which leads him to limited atonement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=1816" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:16</a> | Did Egyptians have the math skills to calculate when new moons or eclipses would occur? | He says they could track some lunar cycles but did not reliably calculate eclipses until much later, and Egyptian lunar records can be unreliable. | He says Egyptian eclipse references often sound like surprise observations rather than predictions, and reliable eclipse calculation does not appear until much later. For the moon, he thinks Egyptians had some expectation of lunar phases, but he also notes that some records appear to have been filled in without observation, creating problems for dating. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=2077" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:37</a> | Why do many devout Christian young adults reject the faith at university, and what can churches do? | He points to young-adult peer-group pressures and says churches need to prepare youth before that developmental stage. | He locates the danger point especially around ages 20 to 24, when young adults are selecting new peer groups and often value current peers over earlier communities. Because many deconversions begin by then, he argues churches should prepare young people earlier, before university and early-adult social pressure become decisive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=2939" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:59</a> | Does forgiving others mean not demanding restitution or damages after a crime? | He says forgiveness and restitution are related but distinct, so pursuing lawful restitution need not violate forgiveness. | He says forgiveness and restitution are connected because a repentant offender should want to make things right. If a Christian offender is truly sorry and willing to pay medical costs outside court, prosecution may be unnecessary; if not, seeking restitution through lawful channels is not the same as withholding forgiveness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=3390" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:30</a> | How would he go about getting churches to unify when Christians in the United States seem to fight each other so much? | He says Christians should be one body, but unity is blocked when people misrepresent what other traditions believe. | He begins from the New Testament language of one baptism, one church, one faith, one Christ, and one resurrection, so he thinks Christian unity is a real obligation. The practical barrier he stresses is polemical misrepresentation: Protestants, Catholics, and Eastern Orthodox often attack caricatures instead of understanding what the other side is actually saying. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=3532" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:52</a> | What did Gavin Ortlund and Trent Horn get wrong specifically? | He says Ortlund overgeneralized Eastern Orthodoxy from one source, while Horn confused sola scriptura with nuda scriptura. | He says Gavin Ortlund treated one Eastern Orthodox source as if it represented Eastern Orthodoxy universally, which misunderstands Orthodox polity. He says Trent Horn's critique of sola scriptura confuses it with nuda scriptura: sola scriptura can use church tradition while treating Scripture as the final authority, not the only authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=4348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:28</a> | Is icon veneration why Russian priests bless military equipment? | He says Russian priests do bless military equipment, but much of it is state posturing rather than sound theology. | He acknowledges that Russian priests do bless military equipment, but he frames much of it as posturing for the state rather than a practice with strong theological justification. He uses the answer as part of a larger point that Christians need to understand one another's theology accurately before arguing about it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=4429" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:49</a> | If the church had remained Jewish, would the issue of icons even have been discussed? | He says no, and adds that icons were not present in the church for the first several centuries but were a later accretion. | He answers no and appeals to early Christian history, saying icons were absent for roughly the first five centuries and begin appearing only later, around the fifth or sixth century. He therefore describes icons as an accretion, something added to the church rather than present from the beginning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=4499" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:59</a> | Is there a connection between Genesis 33:10 and Genesis 41:45? | He says the question would need to be more specific, then reads the passages and gives only a limited answer. | He reads both passages but says he does not see the connection the questioner has in mind. Because the question is too unspecific, he does not attempt a speculative answer and asks for a clearer framing if they want him to address it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=4587" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:27</a> | Where in the ancient Near East is blood described as black? | He points to Semitic literature where spilled blood on the ground can be described as black. | He says he would need to look up exact references, but he has encountered the idea in Semitic and Hebrew-class contexts, especially with blood fallen on the ground or battlefield blood. His explanation is physical as well as literary: blood in the body is red, but pooled or dried blood can look black. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=4798" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:58</a> | When did Norse and Greek myths develop? | He says he does not know, since Greek and Scandinavian myth are outside his area. | He declines to answer because the question is outside his expertise. He says he is not a classicist and is not an expert in Scandinavian myth, so he cannot responsibly date the development of Norse or Greek myth traditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=4848" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:48</a> | Were shepherds liked in Late Bronze Age Egypt? | He says Egyptians generally disliked Semitic shepherds because they were seen as nuisances who fouled waterways and damaged resources. | He says Late Bronze Age Egyptians generally disliked shepherds, especially Semitic goat and sheep herders, because they were viewed as nuisances. They were thought to foul waterways, trespass on land, and consume silage or resources intended for cattle, giving them a poor reputation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=5057" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:17</a> | How would an Egyptian father react if a child wanted to become a shepherd? | He says a non-shepherd father would not react well, since fathers usually tried to keep children in the family trade or a higher-status role. | He says a shepherd father would accept it, but a father from another occupation would likely be unhappy. Egyptian fathers generally tried to place children into the family business or related posts, such as priesthood, farming, scribal work, administration, or inherited titles, so shepherding would be a status drop for many families. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=5360" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:20</a> | What Ramesside document mentions the Yam Suph? | He points to Papyrus Anastasi III and the Onomasticon of Amenemope as texts that mention the sea and reeds. | He identifies Papyrus Anastasi III and the Onomasticon of Amenemope as relevant sources. He explains the connection through Egyptian terms for the sea and reeds, saying Egyptian tui, reeds, is related to the Hebrew suph in Yam Suph. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=5407" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:07</a> | Who redirected the Pelusiac Estuary? | He says no person redirected it; it naturally silted up and the river shifted as a result. | He says the Pelusiac branch was not redirected by a person but clogged with Nile silt. The annual inundation brought silt that was agriculturally useful, but over time some branches could become so choked that water stopped flowing there and the river course shifted naturally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=5507" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:47</a> | Was the Aswan Dam a big mistake? | He says yes and no, noting both benefits and serious consequences such as loss of Nile silt. | He presents the dam as a tradeoff. It stopped the annual silt replenishment, forcing artificial fertilizer or manure, but it also generates major hydroelectric power, reduces floods that displace people, and stores water against drought years, so he does not treat the answer as simply yes or no. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=5755" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:55</a> | Did his Shroud experiment work? | He says it is still going and they have not finished it yet. | He gives only a status update: the experiment is still underway and has not been completed. He says they will report how it went after it is finished, so the transcript does not support a conclusion about success or failure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=5775" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:15</a> | How should Proverbs 29:18 be explained? | He says the couplet should be read together: the "vision" is the law or moral direction that keeps people from being unrestrained. | He says Proverbs 29:18 is a poetic couplet and should not be interpreted from only the first half. "Where there is no vision" means the people are unfettered or let loose, and the balancing line about keeping the law shows that the "vision" is moral and covenantal direction that restrains conduct. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=5966" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:26</a> | Can he explain Genesis 1:1 through Revelation 22:21? | He jokingly summarizes the whole Bible as an arc from creation to crucifixion, resurrection, and consummation. | He treats the request humorously and compresses the whole Bible into one sentence. His summary traces a movement from creation through crucifixion and resurrection to final consummation, explicitly signaling that this is a playful, ultra-short answer rather than a detailed biblical theology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=6030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:30</a> | What do we know about Edom's god Qos? | He says almost nothing is known, apart from limited attestations such as seals and non-religious inscriptions. | He says there are no substantial religious texts for Qos and very little information overall. The god appears in seal impressions or bullae and possibly one monumental inscription, but because those are not explanatory religious texts, they tell scholars very little about Qos himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=6108" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:48</a> | If someone gets arrested in Egypt, what would happen to them? | He warns that Egyptian detention can be harsh, but says tourist police usually prefer to remove or warn tourists rather than make arrests. | He warns that actual arrest can involve harsh detention and lack of legal help, but then distinguishes that from ordinary tourist-police behavior. Tourist police usually do not want arrests because it harms tourism; they would rather escort someone away, warn them, and get them to behave unless the person insists on causing trouble. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=6475" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:55</a> | What are his feelings about buying small antiquities online? | He advises caution because authenticity and looting history are uncertain, and legal purchase usually depends on pre-1970 provenance. | He says online purchases are risky because buyers cannot be sure the item is genuine or unlooted. Legal purchase usually requires documentation showing the object was out of its source country before the 1970 UNESCO framework, so reputable antiquities shops sell the object together with provenance paperwork. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=6715" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:55</a> | What were the Egyptian names of Avaris and its port? | He corrects himself to Hwt-wart for Avaris, the Great Walls, and says the port was Perunefer. | He first thinks of a "good walls" form, then checks himself and corrects Avaris to Hwt-wart, "the Great Walls." He identifies the port as Perunefer and glosses it in terms of good or happy voyages. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=6982" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:22</a> | How did deportation work in ancient Egypt? | He says formal deportation was not typical; Egypt more often excluded, expelled, punished, or executed people. | He says Egypt did not typically operate with formal deportation as a normal category. They might refuse entry, demand that someone leave, beat or punish the person, or execute someone for serious offenses; he treats Pharaoh's expulsion of Israel as exceptional rather than a routine legal process. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=7052" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:32</a> | Is this the layout of the Exodus crossing, with Israelites entering Sinai through dry land? | He says the Israelites crossed dry land but corrects parts of the proposed route and sequence. | He says the Israelites did cross dry land and went south from Pi-Ramesses across the Wadi Tumilat, but he corrects the geography by noting that sites such as Migdol and much of the estuary are already in Sinai. He rejects the idea that they crossed back into Egypt through the sea of reeds as stated in the proposed layout. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=7293" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:33</a> | What was manna, and can we find something like it today? | He says the word itself means something like "what is this," then discusses possible natural analogues cautiously. | He emphasizes that the name itself signals uncertainty, roughly "what is this stuff." He notes that manna appeared on the ground in the morning in a savannah or semi-arid ecosystem, then cautiously looks for analogues among things that could grow or appear overnight, while treating the identification as speculative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=8047" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:07</a> | Was the man ever named Adam in Genesis 2-3? | He says the text mainly calls him "the man," with the definite noun functioning like a designation. | He agrees that Genesis 2-3 commonly refers to him as "the man," and similarly to the woman as "the woman." He explains these as definite nouns that can function like designations in the narrative, while also noting that how such labels sound depends on culture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=8200" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:40</a> | What were the repercussions when elites killed peasants or lower-class people in ancient Egypt? | He says it was against the law even for elites or Pharaoh to kill arbitrarily, though abuses could happen. | He says Egyptian elites were not legally free to kill peasants, and even Pharaoh could not arbitrarily kill someone as a norm. He treats Pharaoh's killing of Israelite male infants as tyrannical and contrary to Egyptian order, not as a normal royal prerogative, even though rulers could ignore the law in practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=8413" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:13</a> | How are gaps in Egyptian king lists filled in? | He says scholars piece together evidence from sources such as the Turin Canon, tomb scenes, burial succession, and synchronisms. | He says scholars use a collection of evidence rather than one single list, including the Turin Canon, papyri, and monuments. He gives succession examples where burial scenes or claims matter, such as the idea that the one who buries the former king inherits, and uses tomb imagery like the opening of the mouth ceremony as succession evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=8602" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:22</a> | If Moses were alive today, could he have a conversation with a modern Coptic speaker? | He says Moses would have spoken Late Egyptian, while Coptic has changed syntax and Greek vocabulary, so mutual understanding would be difficult. | He says Moses would most likely have known Late Egyptian, while Coptic is descended from later Egyptian but has moved on syntactically and lexically. Because roughly a tenth of Coptic vocabulary is Greek and the syntax differs, he thinks direct conversation would be difficult without study. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=8921" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:41</a> | How is HALOT laid out: dictionary order or conjugations? | He says HALOT is a lexicon laid out in Hebrew alphabetic order, not by conjugation. | He identifies HALOT as a lexicon, so it is arranged like a dictionary in Hebrew alphabetic order, beginning with aleph, beth, gimel, daleth, and so on. It is not organized by conjugation, and he still regards it as a top Old Testament lexicon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=8973" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:33</a> | Should Christians use biblical prophecies such as "pierced for our transgressions" and the suffering servant? | He says yes, but churches need to explain the biblical vocabulary and enculturate people into its meaning. | He says such language is appropriate within ancient Near Eastern poetics, but modern readers may not naturally understand it. The church's failure, in his view, is not the vocabulary itself but a failure to enculturate people into the Bible's own language, definitions, and conceptual world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=9236" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:56</a> | What are his thoughts about feelings versus reasoning in scripture and belief? | He says reasoning should channel feelings, and both mind and affections need to be submitted to Christ. | He rejects a simple opposition between reason and feeling. Reason can guide and channel feelings, but salvation and discipleship involve the whole person, so both the mind and affections must be submitted to Christ rather than treating either intellect or emotion as spiritually separate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=9601" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:01</a> | What does his average daily schedule look like? | He says it varies by day, with channel work, email and comments, writing, articles, and projects filling an approximately 80-hour work week. | He says he does not really have one average day. Saturday, Sunday, and often Monday are for channel work such as writing, recording, and editing; Monday and Tuesday also include email and comments; Wednesday and Thursday often go to books, articles, and other cultural-engagement projects, with prayer and Bible reading at the start of the day. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=9855" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:15</a> | Did Egyptians interact with the Hebrews differently or see them as different from other nearby nations? | He says not really; Egyptians tended to treat Israel and Judah as small kingdoms with chiefs, unlike great-power kings called brothers. | He says Egyptians did not seem to treat Israel and Judah as fundamentally different from other small neighboring kingdoms. In Egyptian diplomatic language, small rulers might be called chiefs, while major powers such as Hatti, Babylon, Mitanni, or later Assyria could be addressed as brother-kings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=10044" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:24</a> | Are critical scholarship and critical scholars a threat to Christianity? | He says critical scholarship is not inherently a threat, though atheists or fundamentalists can misuse it; readers should weigh critical, confessional, and historical scholarship. | He says critical scholars vary like anyone else: some oppose Christianity, many do not, and much critical scholarship is valuable. He argues that a well-formed view of the biblical text should consult critical scholarship alongside confessional and historical work, evaluating arguments rather than dismissing whole categories. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=10313" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:53</a> | Did Egyptians see themselves as more civilized than their neighbors? | He says yes, citing Egyptian attitudes in stories such as Sinuhe and in ideas like the Nine Bows and lands outside Egypt as uncivilized. | He answers strongly yes and points to Egyptian literature such as the tale of Sinuhe, where life outside Egypt is contrasted with Egyptian order and civilization. He also refers to Egyptian ideological categories for foreign lands, including concepts such as the Nine Bows, as evidence of a civilizational hierarchy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=11180" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:20</a> | What is his take on the bridegroom of blood event in Exodus 4:24-26, and could Moses have had a partial circumcision? | He says the issue is Moses's neglect of the Abrahamic covenant for his sons, with Zipporah acting as a righteous Gentile who restores covenant faithfulness. | He treats Exodus 4:24-26 as an excursus interrupting the surrounding narrative and says the problem is covenant neglect, not a random attack. Moses has failed to keep the Abrahamic sign for his sons, and Zipporah acts to restore covenant obedience, functioning as the righteous outsider who does what Moses should have done. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=11692" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:52</a> | Is the land in Genesis 2:13 better read as Kish or Gish rather than Kush? | He argues for Gish/Kish in Mesopotamia and says the surrounding geography fits Mesopotamian locations. | He says the reading should be Gish or Kish, not Kush, because the Genesis 2 geography points to Mesopotamian locations. He argues that Havilah and the relevant rivers belong in that Mesopotamian setting, including a Pishon identified by some with a course west of the Euphrates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=12167" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:47</a> | Is he the David Falk who wrote chapter 24 in Behind the Scenes of the Old Testament, and will he write the essay for the questioner? | He says yes, he wrote the chapter, but no, he will not write someone else's essay; he may clarify things if asked. | He confirms that he is the David Falk who wrote chapter 24, but he refuses the joking request to write the student's essay. He says he might provide an insider quotation or clarify issues if asked, but the student still has to do the assignment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=12698" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:38</a> | In Jude 14-15, is Jude quoting 1 Enoch or independently inspired to write that? | He says Jude is quoting 1 Enoch, and that Jude's reading was part of God's inspiration process without making 1 Enoch itself inspired. | He says Jude is quoting 1 Enoch and that this is not controversial in his view. New Testament writers read widely, including Greek philosophers and apocryphal literature, and God can inspire Jude's use of that material without implying that 1 Enoch as a whole is inspired Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=13070" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:50</a> | Could Jude 6 refer to the angels who sinned? | He says Jude must be read as a structured passage and likely refers to angels who rebelled by dereliction of duty. | He says Jude 4-8 is highly structured Greek and has to be read as a unit, with parallel clauses shaping the meaning. Within that structure, he reads Jude 6 as referring to angels who abandoned their appointed place or duty, so the answer depends on the passage's internal argument rather than an isolated phrase. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=13564" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:46:04</a> | Can Old Testament prophecy be used for apologetics? | He says yes, if interpreted properly as ancient Near Eastern poetry, and recommends Robert Alter's work on biblical poetry. | He says yes, but only if Old Testament prophecy is interpreted as poetry in its ancient Near Eastern mode rather than flattened into modern literalism or Western poetic assumptions. He recommends Robert Alter's work on biblical poetry because understanding compressed poetic language is what makes apologetic use responsible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=13682" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:02</a> | Can he describe the Red Sea slave trade? | He says the sources are limited, but slaves moved like other commodities through capture, resale, and final purchase, with receipts preserving evidence. | He says documentation is limited, but the trade worked like other commodity chains: people were captured in war or otherwise enslaved, sold to intermediaries, and then resold to final buyers. He compares the process to the Joseph story, where captivity begins before the later resale. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GhhX7GR-zvE?t=14176" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:16</a> | How many languages can he read, and what advice does he have for learning languages? | He says he reads about 14 languages and recommends focusing on one beginner language for a year before layering in another. | He says he reads about fourteen languages and stresses that reading comprehension is its own skill, distinct from speaking or oral comprehension. His advice is to concentrate on one language for about a year, build real reading ability, and only then layer in another language while continuing to deepen the first. |
